Article Title: Nominee vs legal heir: What is the difference and who has claim on assets? All you need to know
Article Text:
AI image

Who is a Nominee?



Who is a Legal Heir?

Difference between a Nominee and a Legal heir

Aspect Nominee Legal Heir Role Custodian Owner Transfer of Assets Can be done without any legal formalities. Requires testamentary documents for proof of ownership. Powers No power to transact, use, or transfer assets. Power to use and transact assets as an owner. Appointment Owner can appoint anyone, but some institutions specify certain conditions. Determined by succession/personal laws. Legal Right Cannot contest ownership legally. Can contest ownership in a court of law.

When individuals open bank accounts, invest in mutual funds, or buy insurance, they are often asked to name a nominee.However, many people mistakenly assume that the nominee automatically inherits the assets. In reality, the nominee is not always the final recipient — the ownership rights belong to the legal heir, reported ET.Understanding the distinction between these two is essential to ensure that your wealth is passed on to the intended beneficiaries.A nominee acts as a temporary custodian of the deceased person’s assets.The nominee’s role is limited to holding and managing the assets until they are legally transferred to the rightful heirs.According to ET, the nominee cannot sell, trade, or make any transactional decisions about the assets.The account or policy owner can appoint anyone — family, friends, or acquaintances — as a nominee, though financial institutions often prefer close family members.In some cases, the nominee and the legal heir can be the same person, which helps in ensuring a smoother transfer of assets.The legal heir or beneficiary is the rightful owner of the assets once the original holder passes away.To claim ownership, the legal heir must produce valid documents such as a will or other testamentary proof.If there is no will — a situation known as dying intestate — the heir is determined as per the country’s succession or personal laws, reported ET.While a nominee can initially access the assets, only the legal heir can claim them permanently or challenge ownership in court.Experts quoted by ET emphasise that having clarity on this difference helps avoid disputes and ensures that the savings reach the intended family members or beneficiaries.Therefore, it is advisable to regularly update both nominees and wills to align with one’s estate planning goals.
Article Summary: A nominee acts as a temporary custodian of the deceased person’s assets. The legal heir or beneficiary is the rightful owner of the assets once the original holder passes away. To claim ownership, the legal heir must produce valid documents such as a will.

Article Title: Life Certificate 2025: Step-by-step guide to generate and submit Jeevan Pramaan online and offline
Article Text:
What is a Life Certificate and why does it matter



Annual submission deadline for pensioners in 2025



1 November and 30 November.

How to generate Jeevan Pramaan online: Step-by-step process



Step 1: Biometric device registration

Step 2: Authentication and certificate generation

Electronic delivery and access of Digital Life Certificate



How can you submit a Life Certificate in 2025



Offline submission at a bank branch or CSC centre

Online submission via Jeevan Pramaan Portal

Submission through the UMANG App

Who needs to submit the Life Certificate



Central and State Government pensioners

Defence pensioners

Family pensioners in the event of the pensioner’s death

Pensioners from public sector undertakings

Key details required before submitting the Life Certificate



Aadhaar Number

Registered Mobile Number linked with Aadhaar

Pension Payment Order (PPO) Number

Bank Account Number where the pension is credited

Name of the Pension Sanctioning Authority

Is Aadhaar mandatory for a digital Life Certificate



Legal validity of Jeevan Pramaan



How Jeevan Pramaan simplifies pensioner verification



Every year, lakhs of pensioners across India must prove that they are still alive to continue receiving their pension. This verification, known as the Life Certificate or Digital Life Certificate (DLC), is a crucial and mandatory step in ensuring that pension payments are made only to eligible recipients, preventing fraud and misuse.Traditionally, this process involved visiting the bank or pension office in person, standing in long queues, and submitting a signed paper certificate. However, with the government’s focus on digitisation and technological advancement, this process has become much simpler, faster, and more convenient. Pensioners can now submit their certificates digitally from the comfort of their homes using Jeevan Pramaan, a secure, user-friendly, and Aadhaar-based platform.A Life Certificate is an official document that confirms a pensioner is alive. Without it, pension payments are automatically paused by the pension disbursing agency. This verification is essential for preventing fraudulent claims and ensuring that pensions reach the rightful beneficiaries.The Digital Life Certificate (DLC), also known as Jeevan Pramaan, is an advanced biometric-enabled version of the traditional paper certificate.Introduced by the Government of India, it uses Aadhaar-based biometric authentication using either fingerprint or iris scan to verify a pensioner’s identity. Once verified, a digital certificate is automatically generated and shared with authorised pension disbursing agencies.This online process eliminates the need for physical verification, providing elderly pensioners with a simpler and more accessible way to maintain their pension benefits.According to PIB, The submission of the Life Certificate is mandatory for all pensioners who wish to continue receiving their pension without interruption. For the year 2025, the standard submission period is betweenTo make the process more comfortable for the elderly, the government allows pensioners aged 80 years and above to begin the submission process earlier, from 1 October 2025. This extended window gives senior citizens more time and flexibility to complete the procedure without facing last-minute difficulties.The Jeevan Pramaan process involves two main steps: registering the biometric device and generating the digital certificate.Before using the service, pensioners or operators must register their biometric device such as a fingerprint or iris scanner. During registration, the user provides their Aadhaar number and mobile number, after which an OTP (One-Time Password) is sent to their mobile. After entering the OTP, they must provide their name and email ID before scanning their fingerprint or iris.Once authentication is complete, the system confirms that device registration was successful, allowing the user to move to the next step.In this step, the pensioner enters personal details such as Aadhaar number, mobile number, name, PPO number, pension type, sanctioning authority, disbursing agency, and bank account details. The system also asks whether the pensioner has remarried or is re-employed.After entering all details, the pensioner scans their fingerprint or iris using the registered biometric device. Upon successful authentication, the Digital Life Certificate is generated, and an SMS acknowledgment with the Jeevan Pramaan Certificate ID is sent to the registered mobile number.This certificate is automatically stored in the Life Certificate Repository, accessible anytime to both pensioners and pension agencies.Once the Digital Life Certificate is created, it can be electronically delivered to the concerned Pension Disbursing Agency (PDA). PDAs can log in to the Jeevan Pramaan portal to view, verify, or download the certificate directly.Pensioners can also download a PDF copy of their certificate by visiting the Jeevan Pramaan website and entering their Pramaan ID or Aadhaar number. This ensures transparency and easy access for future reference.The government provides several options to submit the Life Certificate, allowing pensioners to choose the method that suits them best.Pensioners who prefer traditional methods can visit their bank branch or a Citizen Service Centre (CSC) to submit their Life Certificate physically. Trained staff at these centres assist pensioners in verifying their details and completing the process.For those unable to travel due to age or health issues, India Post Payments Bank (IPPB) offers a doorstep banking service. Pensioners can schedule an appointment, and an IPPB representative will visit their home to complete the submission using biometric authentication. This service is available for both IPPB and non-IPPB account holders.For pensioners comfortable with technology, the Jeevan Pramaan portal provides a completely online process. By visiting https://jeevanpramaan.gov.in, pensioners can submit their Digital Life Certificate using a computer, laptop, or smartphone equipped with a fingerprint or iris scanner.The system uses the Aadhaar platform to authenticate biometric details. Once verification is successful, a Digital Life Certificate is generated and stored in a secure online repository. Pension disbursing agencies can then directly access the certificate, removing the need for physical paperwork.The UMANG (Unified Mobile Application for New-age Governance) app offers another easy option. Pensioners can download the app from the Google Play Store, search for “Jeevan Pramaan,” and select “Generate Life Certificate.”After entering the necessary details such as Aadhaar number, PPO number, and type of pension, pensioners must authenticate themselves using biometric verification. The app then generates a Digital Life Certificate (DLC), which is automatically shared with the pension disbursing authority.All pensioners who receive government, defence, or private sector pensions through authorised disbursing agencies are required to submit their Life Certificate every year. This includes:Failure to submit the certificate within the stipulated period may result in temporary suspension of pension payments until verification is completed.To ensure a smooth submission process, pensioners should keep certain details and documents ready. These include:Having these details ready helps to avoid delays or errors during authentication, whether submitting online or offline.Yes, an Aadhaar number or Virtual ID (VID) is mandatory to generate a Digital Life Certificate. The entire Jeevan Pramaan system is based on Aadhaar authentication, which ensures accuracy and prevents fraud. Pensioners must make sure their Aadhaar is linked with their mobile number and bank account before attempting to generate the certificate.The Digital Life Certificate is fully valid and legally recognised under the Information Technology Act. Pension disbursing authorities across India accept it as an official document, equivalent to the traditional paper-based life certificate.Its biometric verification system makes it secure and tamper-proof, reducing the chances of wrongful pension disbursal or misuse of pension funds.Before the introduction of Jeevan Pramaan, pensioners often faced difficulties in submitting their certificates, especially those with health problems or limited mobility. The digital system has removed these challenges, allowing pensioners to submit their verification anytime and anywhere.By integrating biometric technology and Aadhaar authentication, Jeevan Pramaan has streamlined pension management, improving efficiency for both pensioners and government departments. It also enhances transparency and ensures that genuine beneficiaries receive payments on time.
Article Summary: A Life Certificate is an official document that confirms a pensioner is alive. Without it, pension payments are automatically paused by the pension disbursing agency. This verification is essential for preventing fraudulent claims and ensuring that pensions reach the rightful beneficiaries. The Digital Life Certificate (DLC), also known as Jeevan Pramaan, is an advanced biometric- enabled version of the traditional paper certificate.

Article Title: What are gold, silver hallmarking charges? Check details and importance
Article Text:
What is gold, silver hallmarking?



Hallmarking charges



ALSO READ |

Testing for purity



The importance of hallmarking



Old hallmarking signs



ALSO READ |

Hallmark symbols



HUID in the hallmarked gold jewellery



Key components of hallmark on gold jewellery



Gold, silver hallmarking : Gold and silver jewllery have long been treasured for their beauty and value. However, ensuring their purity and authenticity is important, especially in a market prone to fraudulent practices. This is where the hallmarking scheme comes into play. Here’s what you need to know about hallmarking charges and its procedures:Hallmarking is the process of accurately determining and officially recording the proportionate content of precious metals in articles, such as gold and silver.It serves as a guarantee of their purity or fineness. In India, hallmarking is governed by stringent standards outlined in IS 15820, according to an ET report.Hallmarking charges are applied per article, regardless of its weight. For gold articles, the charge is Rs 45 per article, with a minimum charge of Rs 200 for a consignment. Similarly, for silver articles, the charge is Rs 35 per article, with a minimum charge of Rs 150 for a consignment. It's important to note that additional taxes and levies may apply.A consignment refers to a collection of articles with the same fineness, from which a sample is drawn and assayed for compliance. Different lots may exist within a consignment.Anyone can have their hallmarked jewellery tested for purity at an A&H centre by paying a testing fee of Rs. 200 (for testing using the fire assay method as per IS 1418). These centres must be recognized by the BIS. The list of recognized A&H centres is available on the BIS website under the hallmarking section.The primary objective of hallmarking is to safeguard consumers from counterfeit products. To achieve this, the Indian government made hallmarking mandatory for gold jewellery from June 16, 2021. Since April 1, 2023, jewellers are prohibited from selling any gold jewellery without a 6-digit Hallmark Unique Identification ( HUID ) number.If you bought gold jewellery on or before June 30, 2021, it may have old hallmarking signs. These signs include the purity/fineness mark and the BIS emblem, along with two additional indications: the jeweller's identification number and the assay centre's mark/number.However, starting July 1, 2021, these two indications were replaced with a unique alphanumeric HUID code. The hallmark remains valid for the lifetime of the jewellery, and consumers can sell old jewellery, whether hallmarked or un-hallmarked, to jewellers.The HUID, or hallmark Unique Identification number, is a key component of hallmarking in gold jewellery. It's a unique six-digit alphanumeric code assigned to each hallmarked item, allowing for traceability. Customers can verify the HUID number using the 'Verify HUID' feature in the BIS Care App.Since the introduction of the HUID on July 1, 2021, the hallmark on gold jewellery consists of three marks: the BIS logo, the purity of the article in caratage and fineness, and a unique six-digit alphanumeric HUID number.
Article Summary: Hallmarking is the process of accurately determining and officially recording the proportionate content of precious metals in articles, such as gold and silver. In India, hallmarking is governed by stringent standards outlined in IS 15820. Since April 1, 2023, jewellers are prohibited from selling any gold jewellery without a 6-digit Hallmark Unique Identification number.

Article Title: Want to create a will? You can create it online as well - Here’s how
Article Text:
If you are planning to create a will, many online platforms offer easy-to-use tools and resources, allowing you to draft and finalise a will without leaving your home. (AI image)

Creating a will online: If you are planning to create a will, many online platforms offer easy-to-use tools and resources, allowing you to draft and finalise a will without leaving your home. Article Title: How to get your CIBIL credit score without a PAN card
Article Text:
Your credit score is a three-digit number that reflects your creditworthiness and repayment capacity.

What is a CIBIL score?

How to get your CIBIL Credit score without a PAN Card

Factors affecting your CIBIL score

CIBIL credit score : Understanding your creditworthiness is pivotal in today's financial landscape, especially when seeking loans or credit cards. The Credit Information Bureau (India) Limited, commonly known as CIBIL, plays an important role in this regard by providing credit scores to borrowers.According to ET, credit score helps banks make informed decisions about extending credit cards and loans. Your credit history influences your CIBIL score , which is calculated using a special method.Your CIBIL score is a three-digit number that reflects your creditworthiness and repayment capacity. Ranging from 300 to 900, a higher score indicates a stronger likelihood of loan approval. Banks and financial institutions use this score to assess the risk associated with lending money.1. Go to the official CIBIL website and click on 'Personal CIBIL score,' then 'Get Your Free CIBIL score.' Follow the link provided and fill in all the required details.2. If you don’t have a PAN Card, you can use an alternative identification such as Passport, Voter ID, Driver’s License, or Ration Card. Enter the number of the chosen ID.3. Provide your date of birth, PIN code, and select your state. Finally, enter your mobile number and click on ‘Accept and Continue’.4. Authenticate your identity by entering the OTP (One-Time Password) delivered to your mobile device on the next screen. Click 'Continue' to proceed.5. Choose whether you want to link the device with your account. Select 'Yes' or 'No' based on your preference.6. Upon successful registration, you will see a page confirming that 'You have successfully registered!'7. Click on ‘Go to Dashboard’ to access and view your CIBIL score.The factors influencing the new CIBIL score, as stated on the CIBIL website, include:1. Depth of credit: This refers to the duration of your existing credit history, calculated from the opening date of your oldest credit account.2. Long-term trend of outstanding balances: The overall trend of outstanding balances over time affects your score.3. Transaction history on credit cards: How you manage transactions on your credit cards impacts your score.4. Ratio of actual repayment amount to total amount due: This ratio indicates your repayment behavior and influences your score accordingly.5. Number of new accounts opened and number of accounts closed: Opening or closing accounts can affect your score, so managing these actions wisely is important.
Article Summary: Your CIBIL score is a three-digit number that reflects your creditworthiness and repayment capacity. Ranging from 300 to 900, a higher score indicates a stronger likelihood of loan approval. Banks and financial institutions use this score to assess the risk associated with lending money.

Article Title: Star Health deploys AI advisor to aid agents in proposals, claims
Article Text:
File photo

.

Mumbai: Star Health has started using its AI-enabled application ATOM PRO as a digital advisor for agents, aiming to improve policy explanation, speed up issuance, and streamline claims processing. The company is targeting 50% of its claims to be settled using AI in two years.According to the company, the new platform shifts focus from backend complexity to customer-facing clarity, even though it operates on the agent’s device. The application acts as a real-time support tool during policy sales. It lays out product features, premium details, riders, and waiting periods on screen, reducing ambiguity at the point of sale. This addresses a persistent concern among buyers about hidden clauses such as sub-limits or exclusions that typically surface only at the time of claims. By clarifying coverage upfront, the platform aims to reduce disputes arising from misinterpretation.“For customers, buying health insurance should not feel complex or time-consuming. They should be able to understand what a plan offers, compare relevant options, know the waiting periods and see accurate pricing before making a decision. ATOM PRO is helping our agents bring that clarity and speed into the purchase journey, while enabling to serve customers with greater confidence,” said Anand Roy, MD & CEO, Star HealthThe rollout has also compressed policy issuance timelines. According to the company, more than 95% of retail policies are now processed digitally, and 80% are issued within five minutes. This replaces a system that earlier relied on paper forms, manual checks, and multi-day underwriting cycles. Buyers can now complete selection, payment, and receive policy documents in a single sitting.The platform’s backend integration extends to claims. Digital reimbursement modules have increased the share of digital claim submissions from 8% to 21%. Agents can upload documents directly, bypassing physical submission processes and reducing delays in claim registration and processing.
Article Summary: Star Health has started using its AI-enabled application ATOM PRO as a digital advisor for agents. It aims to improve policy explanation, speed up issuance, and streamline claims processing. The company is targeting 50% of its claims to be settled using AI in two years.

Article Title: China beats Elon Musk's 'Jesus-level technology' to launch world's first commercial brain chip
Article Text:
The 'Jesus-level technology'



How China's NEO differs from Neuralink



China has moved ahead of Elon Musk 's 'Jesus-level technology' Neuralink in the race to commercialise brain-computer interface technology, approving what is being described as the world's first commercially available brain chip after successful clinical trials.The coin-sized implant, called NEO, has become the first surgically implanted brain-computer interface (BCI) device to clear clinical testing for commercial use, according to the New York Times report.Developed by researchers at Beijing's Tsinghua University and Shanghai-based Neuracle Technology, the device is expected to enter mass production for China's state-run healthcare system.The first version of NEO is aimed at helping patients with spinal cord injuries and paralysis regain control over parts of their nervous system.But researchers, technology companies and investors see brain-computer interfaces as the beginning of a much larger shift in how humans interact with machines.The development comes as Musk's Neuralink continues human trials in the United States but is yet to receive broad regulatory approval for commercial use.Brain-computer interfaces allow signals generated by the brain to be converted into digital commands that can control computers, devices or prosthetics.Musk has repeatedly promoted the technology's potential to transform lives.Speaking via video link at an event in Israel this week, Musk argued that his company, Neuralink, does not receive enough recognition for its achievements."Restoring control of people who are tetraplegics and restoring sight, I think, are pretty big deals," Musk said. "They're sort of what I might call Jesus-level technologies," he added.Neuralink has said its implants could eventually allow users to type, move a computer cursor and operate devices using only their thoughts.Supporters of the technology envision an even more ambitious future, where people could communicate digitally through thoughts or interact with machines without physical input.One reason analysts believe China has moved ahead more quickly is the design of the NEO implant. Unlike Neuralink's N1 device, which requires electrodes to penetrate the brain's cerebral cortex, NEO is designed to sit between the skull and the brain.Eight sensors are placed against the dura mater, the brain's protective outer membrane reducing the invasiveness of the procedure. The implant captures brain signals and sends them to nearby computers, where they are translated into digital commands.According to reports, 36 patients have already tested the device with positive results. Researchers believe the less-invasive approach may help reduce some of the medical risks traditionally associated with brain implants, the Post reported.Experts say brain-computer interfaces could improve the lives of millions of people suffering from neurological disorders, particularly those affecting movement and speech. Potential applications extend beyond paralysis and spinal injuries to conditions such as Parkinson's disease, epilepsy, stroke and depression.However, the technology has also raised concerns about privacy, security and safety. Griffith University cybersecurity expert Dr David Tuffley said the devices offer enormous potential but also create new risks."Brain implants may sound dystopian, but they are a promising part of neuroscience research," he said. At the same time, he warned that the devices could "theoretically allow hackers to access sensitive neural data, such as patients' thoughts and memories.""Hacking may also enable them to impair a patient's cognitive functions such as the ability to concentrate, or even manipulate motor signals to affect how well they move," he said."That's a scary prospect, especially if these devices become more common," he added. Despite rapid progress, researchers also caution that brain implants still face significant biological hurdles.Neuralink's N1 implant is currently being tested in nine patients. One participant, Audrey Crews, recently described the impact the technology has already had on her life."I tried writing my name for the first time in 20 years. I'm working on it," she wrote on X. "It's humbling to know my journey is helping Neuralink refine this technology, which could one day let millions control devices with their minds," she added.
Article Summary: China has approved what is being described as the world's first commercially available brain chip. The coin-sized implant, called NEO, has become the first surgically implanted brain-computer interface (BCI) device to clear clinical testing for commercial use. NEO is expected to enter mass production for China's state-run healthcare system.

Article Title: Two months after telling US staff "there is no option to opt out," Meta pauses its employee-tracking program; confirms the fear workers had from day one
Article Text:
Meta's MCI tracking tool leaked employee data companywide—now it's paused. (AP Photo/Noah Berger, File)

Meta's MCI spilled data internally, revealing employee activity to all

Why 1,600 Meta employees fought the AI tracking tool

Meta has hit pause on the program that quietly recorded its US employees' keystrokes, mouse clicks and screen content to train its AI—but not before the whole thing leaked. An internal security notice, first reported by WIRED, warned this week that data scooped up by the program had been left sitting in the open, accessible to anyone inside the company.The timing stings: it's been barely two months since Meta told staff there was no way to switch the tracking off.The tool goes by Model Capability Initiative, or MCI, and it landed on US employees' work laptops in April. It logged their inputs and grabbed periodic screenshots, all to teach Meta's AI agents how people actually navigate software—the dropdown menus, the keyboard shortcuts, the small stuff models still fumble.Opting out wasn't an option at launch. After weeks of pushback, Meta softened slightly, letting some staff pause the tracking for 30 minutes at a stretch.The notice went out Monday, after a Meta engineer flagged that MCI data had spilled internally. Per WIRED, the exposure spanned roughly 45,000 hive tables holding employee activity—full prompts and transcriptions, private conversations, even people and performance records.Business Insider, which got screenshots of the incident, reported Meta logged it as a SEV 2, the second rung on its five-point severity scale.Inside the company, people were not amused. "I am incensed," one employee wrote in an internal group, per Business Insider, angry the data hadn't been locked down the way Meta promised. Over in a channel where staff swap jokes, someone dropped a meme: "0 days since our last nonsense."A former employee who'd fought the program called the lapse "a mess," and said it was exactly what workers had been flagging for weeks.MCI drew fire well before it leaked. More than 1,600 employees signed a petition warning the data collection invited security and regulatory trouble, breaches included. Zuckerberg brushed past those concerns, telling staff in leaked audio that AI learns best by "watching really smart people do things"—and that Meta's own workforce beat any contractor it could hire.Meta insists nothing was improperly accessed. "We have carefully designed this program with privacy safeguards," spokesperson Tracy Clayton said, "and while we have no indication at this time that any data was improperly accessed by Meta employees, we're pausing it while we investigate."CTO Andrew Bosworth conceded the rollout missed the bar its own privacy review had set.It caps a brutal stretch for Meta—mass layoffs, an AI reorg Bosworth himself called "atrocious," and a roughly $140 billion AI bill. And it follows a rare note of contrition from the top: in a leaked memo this month, reported by Reuters, Zuckerberg told staff the company had "made mistakes and will almost certainly make more" as it pushes through its AI overhaul.
Article Summary: Meta's AI tracking tool leaked employee data companywide. The tool recorded keystrokes, mouse clicks and screen content to train its AI. More than 1,600 employees signed a petition warning the data collection invited security and regulatory trouble. Meta insists nothing was improperly accessed.

Article Title: Tech millionaire Bryan Johnson says, ‘I don’t plan on dying, like actually I won’t die; I am 47, but biologically my cardiovascular abilities, lung health, telomeres are age 18; this is why I say make
Article Text:
Bryan Johnson

Bryan Johnson claims his biological age is 18



Bryan Johnson shares 41 things he learned after spending millions on chasing longevity



Tech entrepreneur and anti-aging influencer Bryan Johnson , who is known for his longevity experiments and biohacking lifestyle, has made another bold claim: he says he doesn’t plan on dying — ever. Johnson, 47, told followers on X: “We are the first generation that won’t die, like actually won’t die, I don’t plan on dying. ” He argued that advances in biotechnology and health optimisation could allow humans to radically extend lifespans beyond current limits.Despite being 47 years old, Johnson claims that his body functions like that of an 18-year old. “My cardiovascular abilities are age 18, my lung capacity is age 18, my telomeres are age 18,” he said. He further added that his health regimen has effectively reversed his biological age. For those unaware, Johnson has invested millions into anti-aging research and personal health experiments which includes strict diets, medical monitoring, and regenerative therapies. Johnson stresses on the fact that prioritising health is essential for longevity. “This is why I say make health your top priority, because the difference between being healthy and non-healthy when you’re thinking is like night and day,” he added.The remarks made by Johnson reflect on the growing movement among the tech elites who feel that advancements in AI, biotechnology, and regenerative medicine could make death optional. However, the critics dismiss these claims and treat them as speculative, whereas Johnson insists his personal results prove that radical life extension is achievable.Recently, Johnson distilled years of experimentation and millions of dollars spend on anti-aging into a list of 41 simple health hacks. In a post shared on social media platform X (formerly known as Twitter) Johnson wrote, “This is it. Everything learned spending millions on longevity.” Despite his $2 million-a-year anti-aging protocol known as Project Blueprint, Johnson’s advice is surprisingly accessible. The list shared by Johnson stresses on low-cost, everyday habits: sleep well, exercise regularly, eat whole foods, avoid added sugar and also limit risky behaviour such as smoking or texting while driving.Among his top recommendations:* Sleep is “the world’s most powerful drug.”* Stick to a consistent bedtime before midnight.* Avoid screens an hour before bed.* Walk after meals or do air squats.* Lift heavy things and stretch daily.* Drink water, get morning sunlight, and protect skin at midday.* See friends weekly, avoid plastic, and circulate air indoors.* Limit alcohol, finish coffee before noon, and keep social media use in check.
Article Summary: Bryan Johnson, 47, claims his biological age is 18 and he doesn't plan on dying. Johnson has invested millions into anti-aging research and personal health experiments. His $2 million-a-year anti- aging protocol includes strict diets, medical monitoring, and regenerative therapies.

Article Title: Hackers are 'using your friends’ WhatsApp accounts' to take over your computer: Report
Article Text:
Invoices

Bank and account statements

Payment records and debt notices

Users of these countries are at risk



How the WhatsApp desktop and WhatsApp web hacking attack works



How to protect yourself

Security experts urge WhatsApp users to stay vigilant and follow these immediate safety guidelines.

Be cautious of any unexpected attachments sent over WhatsApp, even if they look like they are coming from a close friend or trusted business partner.

Never open files that end in script or executable formats, such as .vbs, .vbe, .exe, .bat, or .js unless you are absolutely certain they are safe.

Cybercriminals are launching a massive global malware campaign by hijacking WhatsApp accounts to break into users’ computers. According to a report by Kaspersky’s Global Research and Analysis Team (GReAT), a sophisticated campaign is targeting users of WhatsApp Desktop and WhatsApp Web . By taking control of standard administrative capabilities on victims’ computers, the hackers can gain complete remote access to the compromised machines.The hacking campaign is a clever social engineering cyber attack as hackers – instead of using random, suspicious phone numbers – send direct messages from WhatsApp accounts they have already broken into. This is due to the fact that since the messages come from a trusted contact, recipients are far more likely to download the attached files quickly.To make the trap even more convincing, the malicious files are disguised as routine business documents that people handle every day, such as:To trick the computer's built-in defenses, the malicious code even contains hidden text and metadata designed to mimic legitimate Microsoft Windows Update components, Kaspersky’s report pointed out.“Attackers are exploiting trust within messaging platforms. The file names are carefully disguised as routine business documents... and localized across multiple languages to support broad targeting. Once opened, they trigger a staged infection chain that silently retrieves and executes additional malicious components,” explained Fareed Radzi, a security researcher at Kaspersky GReAT.The campaign has an international footprint, with localised file names written in English, Portuguese, French, German and Malay.While it has heavily hit European language regions, a significant wave of victims has already been identified across Asia and South America. Affected areas include Brazil, Singapore, Taiwan, and Vietnam, with the highest number of overall infections observed in Malaysia.If a user clicks on the malicious file, it kicks off a silent, multi-stage infection process inside their computer. First, the file launches a hidden script that builds a secret working folder deep within the computer’s public documents.Then, the malware connects to an external server run by the hackers to pull down additional malicious files. Finally, it installs a commercial Remote Monitoring and Management (RMM) software package or tools which the hackers use them to spy on and completely control the victim’s system.
Article Summary: Hackers are hijacking WhatsApp accounts to break into users’ computers. A significant wave of victims has already been identified across Asia and South America. Affected areas include Brazil, Singapore, Taiwan, and Vietnam, with the highest number of infections observed in Malaysia.

Article Title: What is GLM-5.2: China’s AI model challenging Anthropic’s Claude Fable 5 in coding and long-context reasoning
Article Text:
GLM-5.2: A Chinese AI system designed for extended memory and coding



Inside GLM-5.2’s strategy for making long prompts computationally viable



Notable gains in coding performance and agentic task execution

Strong long-horizon reasoning enabled by a 1M-token context window

Two reasoning modes: GLM-5.2 (max) for peak capability and GLM-5.2 (high) for balanced performance and token efficiency

Released with MIT-licensed open weights for broad accessibility

API pricing remains unchanged from GLM-5.1

How GLM-5.2 performs in software engineering evaluations



How GLM-5.2 uses open access to differentiate in a closed model era



Elon Musk’s comment and the wider argument about timing



What GLM-5.2 actually changes in practice



In recent days, a new large language model from China has started circulating through technical circles with an unusual mix of curiosity and skepticism attached to it. The Chinese model, GLM-5.2, comes from Zhipu AI and has been positioned as a serious step forward in long-context reasoning and coding-heavy workloads. Much of the attention has not only come from benchmark tables or developer notes, but from the wider argument it has triggered about how quickly China’s frontier AI systems are closing in on the US leaders. As reported by the South China Morning Post (SCMP), comments exchanged online between company figures and Elon Musk have only sharpened that tone. What might have stayed a quiet technical release instead drifted into a broader conversation about capability gaps, open releases, and whether the current hierarchy in advanced AI is as stable as it looks on paper.GLM-5.2 is not presented as a dramatic reinvention so much as a deliberate expansion of what already existed. It sits on top of earlier work from Zhipu AI, stretching the model’s ability to hold and process extremely long inputs while trying to keep coding performance consistent over extended runs.The most striking claim is less about raw intelligence and more about memory span. The system is engineered to work with context windows that stretch into territory where entire codebases, research logs or multi-stage agent tasks can be held in a single session.That scale, often described in millions of tokens, is where much of the engineering effort seems to have gone.Inside the technical breakdown, a recurring theme is cost control. Extending context length is not just a question of adding capacity. It strains memory systems, slows inference, and forces compromises in how information is retrieved inside the model.GLM-5.2 introduces a set of architectural shortcuts meant to keep those pressures manageable. Parts of the attention system are shared across layers, reducing repeated computation. Other adjustments focus on speculative decoding, trying to predict future tokens more efficiently without bloating the system.According to the X (Formerly Twitter) post on, June 16, 2026,The strongest claims around GLM-5.2 sit in coding evaluations. In a range of software engineering benchmarks, it is reported to sit close to leading proprietary systems, including models such as Claude Fable 5 developed by Anthropic, as reported by SCMP.On some coding tasks, it appears to edge ahead of older open models, particularly in long-horizon scenarios where a system has to maintain consistency across multiple steps rather than solving isolated problems. That distinction matters in practice. Many models perform well in short bursts but lose structure when the task stretches.According to official, Z.ai blog, one of the more politically charged aspects of GLM-5.2 is its openness. The model has been released under an open licence, allowing wider access for developers and researchers outside the company’s immediate ecosystem.That decision lands at a time when frontier models are increasingly being restricted or tiered, particularly in the US. Some systems are being partially locked behind API access or downgraded for certain research use cases. Against that backdrop, an open-weight release reads as both technical and strategic positioning.It also feeds into a narrative that has been building around Chinese AI labs: that openness might become a competitive advantage, especially in developer adoption and experimentation, even if the absolute performance edge still tilts elsewhere.The debate widened after remarks from Elon Musk suggesting that a Chinese system could match the latest frontier models from the US within a relatively short timeframe. A response from Zhipu’s leadership pushed back on that timing, hinting that parity might arrive sooner than expected.The exchange itself was brief, almost casual, but it quickly became symbolic. Not because it settled anything, but because it reflected how compressed expectations have become in AI development.GLM-5.2 is designed to sit inside long workflows, hold context without collapsing, and manage iterative coding or research tasks that would normally require repeated resets. That alone places it in a different category of use compared with lighter conversational models. It is less about quick answers and more about sustained involvement in a task that refuses to end neatly.Whether that makes it competitive with the most advanced systems from OpenAI or Anthropic is still debated. But it does suggest a narrowing space between open and closed models, particularly in domains where persistence matters more than short-form reasoning.
Article Summary: GLM-5.2 is a Chinese AI system designed for extended memory and coding. The system is engineered to work with context windows that stretch into territory where entire codebases, research logs or multi-stage agent tasks can be held in a single session. The model has been released under an open licence, allowing wider access for developers and researchers.

Article Title: As companies like Amazon, Uber and Meta restrict AI use for employees, Palo Alto CEO Nikesh Arora offers a solution
Article Text:
Palo Alto Networks CEO Nikesh Arora

Arora bats for three-stop approach



The

AI Business model trap

: LLMs want cash flow to fund the race to AGI or the next model. Enter free consumer AI - they are losing a lot of money on the breadth of models to serve consumers for free! They are caught in the post training data trap, free consumer usage feeds post training needs, it can't be right to stop serving customers for free?

But they need money for the compute:

The monetization challenge is being pointed to Enterprises.

Phase 1 - seemed easy, value capture in coding, the most bottom up motion in enterprise - with low customization per customer. Developers continue to train coding, tasks and eventually will train flawless skills.

Phase 2 is where the challenge lies, showing true enterprise value. The promise of efficiency, accuracy, elimination of resources - that requires a different approach, build depth with harnesses, context, memory, solving for edge cases with deterministic guardrails! Build skill libraries - enter FDEs. Yes,FDEs will train the enterprise Waymos of the world.

The risk - high token pricing for enterprises while consumers for free! Yes for consumer distribution businesses (aka Google, Meta, Apple, etc) it makes sense to hold on the distribution with free AI.

If you want to win enterprise, you should be forward pricing tokens. The cheaper the tokens for enterprises it will allow for experimentation, workflow reimagination - instead CIOs are busy restricting AI use and working on making the use more efficient!

Paradox: They still haven't fully understood and embraced the value of AI in the enterprise.

If I were them:

1. Cut token pricing now, else send enterprises to secure opensource and end up with friction filled routing layers.

2. Show me how enterprises can use their context, training and data as their competitive advantage.

3. Build tools for rapid edge case learning and reducing false positives.

Tech companies like Amazon, Uber, Meta and others that rushed to put artificial intelligence (AI) tools into the hands of every employee are hitting the brakes, introducing usage caps, discouraging unnecessary AI use or pushing workers toward cheaper alternatives in an effort to bring skyrocketing AI spending under control. This has prompted a solution from Palo Alto Networks CEO Nikesh Arora, who took to X (formerly Twitter) to break down what he calls the “AI Business Model Trap.”Arora argues that Large Language Model (LLM) makers are caught in a dangerous cycle: they need massive cash flow to fund the race toward Artificial General Intelligence (AGI), yet they lose enormous sums of money serving everyday consumers with free AI tools to feed their post-training data needs.He said to make up for these consumer losses, tech giants are pointing their monetization challenges squarely at enterprises. Arora warns that this will create a massive risk: charging exorbitant token prices to businesses while consumers get the same models for free.According to Arora, the transition of AI into the corporate world is moving through two problems and for that, he has suggested a three-point problem. The solution will work to stop companies from locking down their AI tools. What he said in his X post:
Article Summary: Palo Alto Networks CEO Nikesh Arora breaks down what he calls the "AI Business Model Trap" Arora says tech giants are charging exorbitant token prices to businesses while consumers get the same models for free. The solution will work to stop companies from locking down their AI tools.

Article Title: People training new AI models admit they just get chatbots to do it, and experts warn this kind of 'AI inbreeding' could make future systems less useful than the companies expect
Article Text:
A remote AI contractor secretly copying answers from a chatbot into an AI training platform. Image Credit: ChatGPT

How human trainers became important for the creation of artificial intelligence

Workers claim the use of AI technologies to carry out AI training assignments

An AI trainer reviews and compares a human-written response with a chatbot-generated one on a desktop monitor. Image Credit: ChatGPT

Importance of the quality of training data

Concerns over ‘

AI inbreeding

’ among researchers

The industry lacks visibility on this issue

Increasing pressure on data pipelines

The approach to creating artificial intelligence has come under question following revelations that some of the people hired to do the job are secretly employing chatbots that already exist to carry out their duties. It has been noted that this could lead to future AI systems being lower in quality due to the inclusion of machine-made material into the human-created datasets.According to reporting by New Scientist , some of the contractors who have been tasked with doing AI training said that they use chatbots like ChatGPT when carrying out conversation-related work and evaluation exercises rather than writing answers themselves.Researchers have warned that this could lead to an ever-increasing number of AI systems being trained on outputs created by previous AI systems.Contemporary large language models do not consist merely of computer code and web-scrapped datasets. There is an essential element of human contractors working with AI models, rating their answers, and providing examples of desirable answers. According to OpenAI , such practice is usually applied in the context of reinforcement learning from human feedback. This makes AI models useful and safe.Organisations like Partnership on AI have underlined the importance of human input in making AI models more reliable and predictable for users.While organizations expand their training facilities, they are forced to rely more on contractor-generated data.According to the New Scientist report, workers said some training assignments are completed with chatbot assistance. Workers do not spend time developing their own responses and evaluations but rather instruct AI technology to generate responses and provide them with their training assignments.Although such an approach increases workers’ efficiency, it raises the question of whether the data generated represents actual human reasoning.AI models pick up on patterns in the training data. Should that training data be faulty, repetitive, or even machine-generated, this could influence the performance of such models in the future.Academics working within the domain of machine learning have already raised alarm bells regarding potential issues associated with training AI on synthetic data. In particular, it is possible for the repetitive iterations of AI-generated content to lead to homogeneity in outputs and the repetition of any mistakes made along the way. This effect is often called “model collapse,” although it has not been definitively proven.Ultimately, the main issue lies in the possibility of AI models being trained on AI-generated data instead of human-generated examples.A few researchers and critics have referred to this feedback system as “AI inbreeding,” where AI models are continuously being trained using data that has been shaped by previous iterations of AI technology.This does not imply that AI models are literally training on themselves, but rather that the output produced by AI technologies is increasingly being used online. In the long run, this may result in a lack of diversity in data.This trend could make models more biased or less able to handle unusual cases that require human insight.AI companies have not disclosed the prevalence of training behavior via chatbots among their contractors. Nevertheless, the problem demonstrates a larger challenge in the creation of AI solutions: the verification of the source of the training dataset.The quality control system is applied to ensure the quality of the contractor’s work; however, it is not always capable of determining whether the reply was written by a human being or created with the help of AI.Thus, it is hard to estimate the volume of the AI-generated material that is present in training datasets.With the increase in complexity of AI models, there is a need for a larger amount of more sophisticated datasets that would be needed for their training and optimization. At the same time, it is becoming increasingly difficult to gain access to good human-created data due to a number of reasons. The situation has led to an increased use of contractor-based workflows and the creation of synthetic data. This has made the necessity for data quality control even higher, according to experts.The findings reported by New Scientist regarding the issue in question have roots in the problem that plagues the field of artificial intelligence concerning the quality of training data as the technology scales.Despite the fact that there are currently no signs pointing to the collapse of the systems because of the problems discussed above, scientists express concerns regarding the possible impact on their performance if not handled properly for a long period of time.At present, according to experts, the major problem lies in preserving human contribution to the training process despite the increasing level of automation.
Article Summary: Workers claim the use of AI technologies to carry out AI training assignments has led to an increased use of contractor-based work. Researchers have warned that this could lead to an ever-increasing number of AI systems being trained on outputs created by previous AI systems. The major problem lies in preserving the human contribution to the training process despite increasing automation.

Article Title: Gigabyte’s X3D Turbo Mode 2.0 boosts gaming FPS by up to 34%
Article Text:

Cyberpunk 2077 – up to 25% gains, averaging 297 FPS

How it works



Compatibility and availability

Gigabyte has unveiled X3D Turbo Mode 2.0, a platform-level performance feature designed to unlock the full potential of AMD Ryzen X3D processors without requiring manual tuning or overclocking expertise. Internal testing shows significant improvements in modern titles:On the productivity side, Cinebench R24 Multi-Thread scores improved by 13.8%, from 2,247 to 2,556.X3D Turbo Mode 2.0 builds on AMD’s 3D V-Cache technology, which accelerates data access for smoother frame rates and responsiveness. At its core is GIGABYTE’s Dynamic OC Engine, paired with a dedicated onboard hardware controller and optimized power delivery design.Unlike traditional overclocking, which simply pushes the CPU harder, the Dynamic OC Engine intelligently adjusts performance behavior based on real-time workload demands. The result is stable, consistent gains across gaming and productivity workloads.The feature is supported exclusively on GIGABYTE X3D Series motherboards, with day-one support for AMD Ryzen 9000, 8000, and 7000 Series X3D processors.Models include:The lineup spans multiple price points, catering to enthusiasts, professional creators, and workstation users.
Article Summary: X3D Turbo Mode 2.0 builds on AMD’s 3D V-Cache technology, which accelerates data access for smoother frame rates and responsiveness. The feature is supported exclusively on GIGABYTE X3D Series motherboards, with day-one support for AMD Ryzen 9000, 8000, and 7000 Series X3d processors.

Article Title: Japanese company Sakana whose Fugu AI model matched Claude Fable 5’s performance shares a message for all countries banned from using Anthropic's most powerful AI models
Article Text:
How Sakana Fugu actually works

Sakana's message to countries locked out by Washington

Japanese company Sakana whose Fugu AI model matched Claude Fable 5’s performance shares a message for all countries banned from using Anthropic's most powerful AI modelsJapan's Sakana AI has built a workaround for one of the AI industry's newest problems: what happens when the US government cuts off access to the world's best models. Its answer is Fugu, a multi-model orchestration system launched on June 22 that the company says matches Anthropic's restricted Claude Fable 5 and Mythos models on benchmarks—without using either of them. The system pools together publicly available models like Google's Gemini 3.1 Pro, OpenAI's GPT-5.5, and Claude Opus 4.8, then dynamically picks and combines them for each task.The timing is no accident. Fugu arrives days after a US export control order forced Anthropic to pull its top models from global use on June 12, locking out entire countries overnight. Sakana's message to those left stranded is blunt: you don't need the banned models to get banned-model performance. CEO David Ha is pitching orchestration not just as a clever engineering trick but as a hedge against the kind of single-vendor dependence that can collapse the moment Washington changes its mind.Fugu, Japanese for pufferfish, is itself a language model trained to call other LLMs from an agent pool, including instances of itself recursively.You send a request to one endpoint, and Fugu decides everything internally—model selection, delegation, verification, and synthesis. It solves simple tasks on its own and assembles a team of expert models when a problem needs more. None of that complexity ever touches your code.Sakana offers two versions through a single OpenAI-compatible API. The base Fugu balances performance with low latency for everyday work and slots into tools like Codex, and you can opt specific agents out of its pool for compliance reasons. Fugu Ultra is the flagship, tuned for hard, multi-step jobs like AI research, cybersecurity analysis, and patent investigations. On Sakana's benchmarks, Fugu Ultra posted 73.7 on SWE-Bench Pro, ahead of Opus 4.8 at 69.2, and 95.5 on the GPQA-D science test. The catch, critics note, is that an orchestrator can only be as good as the models it can reach—and Fugu still trails the restricted Fable 5 on several tougher tasks.For Ha, the export controls are the whole point. He believes the strongest AI systems won't be isolated monoliths but collective intelligences, much like human progress itself. "Human intelligence is fundamentally a collective intelligence," he wrote on X, arguing that the future belongs to systems that learn how to coordinate. Fugu, he says, is a major step toward that. Relying on a single company's model for national infrastructure is a massive risk, he argues, and recent export controls prove access to top models can vanish overnight. Collective intelligence is his answer—a practical hedge against that concentration of power, with Fugu routing around vendor restrictions through an entirely swappable agent pool. "By orchestrating the world's models, we are delivering the resilient blueprint required for AI sovereignty," he wrote, crediting his Tokyo team for shipping it.Not everyone is convinced. Prime Intellect engineer Elie Bakouch called it a closed orchestrator built on closed models, arguing users now control even less than before. Pricing runs steep too, with Fugu Ultra matching GPT-5.5 at $35 per million tokens combined. Fugu is live everywhere except the EU and EEA, where Sakana is still squaring its routing with GDPR.
Article Summary: Sakana AI has built a workaround for one of the AI industry's newest problems: what happens when the US government cuts off access to the world's best models. Sakana's Fugu system pools together publicly available models like Google's Gemini 3.1 Pro, OpenAI's GPT-5, and Claude Opus 4.8, then dynamically picks and combines them for each task.

Article Title: A24's next project after Backrooms: building AI tools with Google's $75 million
Article Text:
A24's Backrooms became its biggest hit ever—now the studio is using that success to fund AI tools with Google.

Backrooms

The first tool is a storyboard generator

A24's fanbase isn't sold

Backrooms

Elden Ring

Fresh off the biggest hit in its history, A24 is taking roughly $75 million from Google and pairing with DeepMind to build AI tools for filmmakers. The Wall Street Journal first reported the figure, which matches what Thrive Capital invested in A24's 2024 round—the one that valued the studio at $3.5 billion. It's the first time Alphabet's Google has taken an equity stake in a film studio, and it lands while, A24's $300 million-plus blockbuster, is still fresh in theatres.The deal is multiyear and nonexclusive, so A24 can still work with other AI firms and DeepMind with other studios. It also keeps Google away from A24's film and TV library. Google cannot train its models on the catalogue, a condition both sides made a point of stating upfront.Work has already started at A24 Labs, the roughly 20-person tech team led by partner Scott Belsky, who came over from Adobe last year. The first project uses AI to generate storyboards—the rough sketches directors use to plan scenes before shooting. Belsky told the Journal the tools "won't look anything like the prompted generation type of AI that people feel uncomfortable with," and argued that most developers pitch AI on the wrong promise: cheaper and faster, when the goal should be better.DeepMind is framing it the same way. CEO Demis Hassabis said the point is to build with artists from the start rather than in isolation, and product VP Eli Collins said breakthroughs come when the technology reaches the best minds in a field. As Wired and Business Insider have noted, that messaging matters—auteur approval is useful currency as Google works to make video models like Veo the ones filmmakers actually want to use.The move puts A24 in line with studios that got there first. Netflix bought Ben Affleck's startup InterPositive in March, Lionsgate is building models with Runway, and Disney's deal with OpenAI fell apart when Sora shut down. But A24 is the one with the most to lose, because its brand runs on emerging directors and a young audience that tends to recoil from AI. Pew found about half of adults under 30 think the technology will harm society, anddirector Kane Parsons—whose film made all that money for the studio—has called generative AI "genuinely harmful."Discontent showed up fast. Within hours, the comment sections under the announcement had filled with fans saying flatly that they were out, some vowing to skip A24 releases altogether. The anger won't show in the numbers right away, but it lands on the reputation A24 has spent a decade building—that of a studio on the artists' side.A24 insists the tools won't make its movies, only assist the people who do. But the line between assisting and making tends to blur once a technology is in the room, and the studio knows it. With Alex Garland's $175 millionadaptation up next, A24 is testing a tricky proposition: whether the audience that prized it for trusting filmmakers over technology will stick around now that the technology is part of the deal.
Article Summary: A24 is taking roughly $75 million from Google and pairing with DeepMind to build AI tools for filmmakers. It's the first time Alphabet's Google has taken an equity stake in a film studio. The first project uses AI to generate storyboards—the rough sketches directors use to plan scenes before shooting.

Article Title: In 1978, two computer hobbyists wired a machine through a Chicago blizzard and built the online message board that helped create internet forums
Article Text:
The IMSAI 8080 is an early microcomputer released in late 1975, based on the Intel 8080 (and later 8085) and S-100 bus. Image Credit: Wikipedia

A frozen night and an idea that works

Ward Christensen with CBBS, the first computer bulletin board system. Image Credit: Wikimedia Commons

Why the early boards were different

From hobbyists to a global online community

During the winter of 1978, there was a heavy snowfall in Chicago, which resulted in the creation of another experiment with regard to communication channels. Two hobbyists who were stuck inside during the storm decided to hook up a hobbyist computer to a telephone line.The developed online environment was referred to as the Computerized Bulletin Board System or CBBS. Its development was driven by immediate practical needs. The developers needed a tool that let users exchange files, post messages, and interact remotely. Before CBBS, computers had been isolated machines. However, with the introduction of CBBS, users would be able to log into the system and post their messages in order to get responses from other users. Although it was not the World Wide Web, it helped lay the groundwork for it.The CBBS story began with a practical problem. Local computer enthusiasts needed a way to share information when they could not meet in person. Christensen and Suess solved the problem by connecting a home computer to a phone line, allowing users to dial in remotely.During the late 1970s, personal computing was still young and highly experimental.The bulletin board system provided those pioneers with something public, even though it was hosted on humble home equipment.What made early bulletin boards unique was not just their ability to save messages, but the way they brought people together. As stated in a research paper, available on PubMed , the analysis of the university bulletin board system showed particular patterns in communication. In particular, this article describes the creation of social networks based on hub members who were actively connecting different forums.The paper shows that computerized bulletin board systems were much more than a way to exchange files. Such systems let users leave messages, wait for replies, and continue conversations over days or weeks. Digital conversations could continue long after a message was posted.This structure helps explain why historians now pay attention to bulletin boards as social spaces. Indeed, as it is mentioned in a digital technology review indexed at PubMed , early social media websites were characterized by simple text-based communication in chat rooms and online forums until they became the current mass communications systems. According to the source, modern social media have their roots in these early systems.Even at that time, an emerging notion of the community based on threads, replies, and sticky messages was coming into view. CBBS’s main appeal was its ease of access. Users did not have to be in the same place as the system administrator. One could connect from afar, leave one's message, and check in again later to read other people's responses. That small change altered how people used computers. It turned a calculating machine into a tool for social communication.Although classic dial-up services became obsolete with the rise of the web in the mid-1990s, CBBS’s basic model lived on. It carried over into the forum sections of modern websites. In conclusion, the 1978 snowstorm shows how many common online practices began as practical solutions created by ordinary users.
Article Summary: The Computerized Bulletin Board System (CBBS) was the first computer bulletin board system. CBBS was developed in 1978 after a heavy snowfall in Chicago. Users could connect from afar, leave one's message, and check in again later to read other people's responses.
